excerpt: Coins have to get initially distributed somehow, and a constant rate seems like the best formula. The fact that new coins are produced means the money supply increases by a planned amount, but this does not necessarily result in inflation.
body:
Source excerpt:
Coins have to get initially distributed somehow, and a constant rate seems like the best formula. The fact that new coins are produced means the money supply increases by a planned amount, but this does not necessarily result in inflation.

---

Cited by leaf "Why a fixed supply matters":
Bitcoin's supply schedule is fixed at 21 million units, approached asymptotically through a sequence of halvings: every ~210,000 blocks (~four years) the per-block subsidy paid to miners is cut in half. The subsidy started at 50 BTC per block in 2009, dropped to 25 in 2012, 12.5 in 2016, 6.25 in 2020, and 3.125 in 2024; it will reach effectively zero by ~2140 [1]. This is not a target or a guideline — it is enforced by every node, every block, with no opt-out.

The interesting question is not 'why 21 million' (the specific number is arbitrary; Satoshi has said as much in correspondence), but 'why fixed at all'. The Austrian answer is that any discretionary monetary authority — even one staffed entirely by competent and well-meaning people — is structurally compromised, because the *option* to expand the supply for short-term political ends is always present and always eventually exercised [2]. A fixed supply removes the option entirely.

The halvings are an interesting design choice on top of the cap. They function as a *Schelling point* for monetary policy expectations: every node operator, every miner, every wallet user knows the next halving date years in advance, knows the new issuance rate, and can plan accordingly. There is no FOMC meeting, no surprise policy shift, no leaked memo — just the published schedule, executed by code [3].

The common objection is that a deflationary money discourages spending and produces hoarding. The Austrian counter is twofold: (a) the relevant deflation is *secular productivity-driven* deflation, which historically tracked nineteenth-century gold-standard economies that nonetheless saw enormous capital investment and growth; and (b) the alleged 'paradox of thrift' confuses cash hoarding with capital formation — savings that are not consumed are precisely what funds investment, regardless of whether they're held in money or in claims on capital. Whether you find this persuasive is a separate question; what is not in dispute is that the supply is in fact fixed, and that this is the design's most consequential single decision.

---

Cited by leaf "UTXO model versus account model":
Bitcoin tracks money as a set of *unspent transaction outputs* (UTXOs), not as account balances. Every transaction takes one or more existing UTXOs as inputs, destroys them, and creates one or more new UTXOs as outputs. There is no global ledger of 'who owns what' — there is only the chainstate database, which is the set of UTXOs that currently exist. A wallet's 'balance' is just the sum of the UTXOs it can spend [1].

This is the most important architectural choice in the protocol after proof of work, and it has consequences in every direction. *Validation* parallelises naturally: two transactions that don't touch the same UTXOs can be validated independently, with no shared state to lock around. *Privacy* improves because there is no canonical 'account' identity attached to a UTXO — addresses are reused only by convention, and well-behaved wallets generate a fresh address per receipt [2]. *Replay protection* is automatic: once a UTXO is spent, the same transaction cannot be replayed because its inputs no longer exist.

The alternative — the account model used by Ethereum and most legacy banking systems — keeps a balance per address. Transactions debit one balance and credit another. This is intuitively familiar and makes some smart-contract patterns easier to express. It also serialises validation (every transaction touches the global account state), creates implicit replay risk (the same nonce-less transaction could be re-broadcast), and produces stronger linkability (every transaction from an address contributes to a single canonical history) [3].

In the chainstate, each UTXO is keyed by `(txid, output_index)` and stores the `scriptPubKey` (the cryptographic puzzle that locks it) and the value in satoshis. The chainstate is what every full node maintains as its authoritative view of the current state of the world; the historical block data is interesting but not necessary for validating new blocks beyond the chainstate. This is what makes pruning possible: a node can discard old blocks once their effects on the chainstate have been applied, and continue validating new blocks indefinitely with a few gigabytes of working state.
